title: Gradient Accumulation
concept: Gradient accumulation simulates a larger effective batch by processing several smaller micro-batches, collecting their gradients, and updating the weights only after those smaller passes are combined.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which training choice most directly raises memory by forcing more examples through the network at once?

options.text: Saving results on a different day

exercises.explain: Exactly. Larger batches often improve training behavior, but they can also exceed memory limits.

cards.title: Build a wall one brick at a time
cards.body: What if you want the steadier learning behavior of a large batch, but your GPU only fits a small one? Gradient accumulation is the workaround: you process several smaller pieces, then combine their learning signal before taking one optimizer step.

cards.title: What accumulation does
cards.body: With gradient accumulation, the model runs forward and backward on a small micro-batch, keeps the resulting gradients, then repeats that process a few times. Only after enough micro-batches have been collected does the optimizer update the weights, creating a larger effective batch without needing that whole batch in memory at once.

cards.title: Add receipts before paying
cards.body: Imagine buying items with a running tab. Instead of paying after every single item, you gather a few receipts, total them up, and then make one payment. Accumulation works similarly: several small gradient contributions are gathered before one update happens.

cards.title: Small GPU, larger effective batch
cards.body: Suppose your GPU fits only 16 examples at once, but you want training behavior closer to batch 64. You can process four micro-batches of 16, accumulate their gradients, and then update once. Peak memory stays close to 16-example training, but the update reflects 64 examples total.

cards.title: It saves memory, not time
cards.body: Gradient accumulation is not a magical speed boost. You still run multiple forward and backward passes, so wall-clock time may increase. Its main value is letting you act like you used a larger batch without needing the full batch in memory at once.

cards.title: Direct large batch vs. accumulated small batches
cards.body: These two approaches can aim for a similar effective batch, but their memory shape is different.

left.text:
Fewer optimizer steps.
Needs enough memory for the whole batch at once.
Can crash if peak VRAM is too high.

right.text:
Processes smaller chunks one by one.
Lower peak memory per pass.
Usually takes more total time per full effective batch.

exercises.prompt: What problem is gradient accumulation mainly trying to solve?

options.text: Wanting a larger effective batch without fitting that whole batch in memory at once.
Wanting to delete the model weights after every example.

exercises.explain: Right. It is a memory workaround for batch size, not a replacement for the whole training process.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Gradient accumulation gives the memory footprint of a small batch while pretending, in effect, to learn from a larger one.
exercises.explain: True. That is the core idea: small passes, larger combined update.

exercises.sentence: Several micro-batches together create a larger effective ___.
exercises.explain: Batch is correct. The model updates only after enough small batches have been combined.

exercises.prompt: Which training plan best matches gradient accumulation?

options.text: Run four small batches, keep their gradients, then update the weights once.
Use one giant batch that already crashes the GPU.
Skip the backward pass to save time.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Accumulation collects several smaller gradient signals before one optimizer step.

exercises.prompt: A researcher wants batch-64 behavior, but the GPU only fits 16 examples at a time. Which choice best fits?

options.text: Process four micro-batches of 16, accumulate gradients, then update once.
Pretend the GPU has more memory and do batch 64 anyway.
Use fewer labels so memory disappears.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Gradient accumulation is the practical way to simulate the larger batch without needing all 64 examples resident at once.
